title: Choosing for Accuracy vs. Speed
concept: Choosing a backbone often means balancing accuracy against inference speed and latency: heavier backbones may extract richer features, while lighter ones often produce a better user experience when fast response matters.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what do backbone families mainly give you?

options.text: Different tradeoff starting points for feature extraction
A guarantee that every model runs equally fast
A way to avoid choosing hardware entirely

exercises.explain: Exactly — and one of the biggest tradeoffs is accuracy versus speed.

cards.title: A right answer that arrives too late can still feel wrong
cards.body: If a camera assistant takes half a second to react, users feel the lag even if the prediction is strong. In many products, speed is part of quality. That is why backbone choice is rarely about accuracy alone.

cards.title: Heavier vs. lighter backbones
cards.body: A heavier backbone usually spends more compute and often aims for stronger feature extraction. A lighter backbone reduces cost so predictions arrive faster. The tradeoff is not absolute, but it is a useful first mental model: more capacity can help accuracy, while less cost can help latency and responsiveness.

cards.title: Truck versus scooter
cards.body: A truck can carry more, but it is not the right choice for weaving through a crowded alley. A scooter carries less, but it is quick and practical in tight spaces. Backbone choice feels similar: some jobs reward raw strength, others reward agility.

cards.title: Batch analysis and live video want different things
cards.body: A cloud service that analyzes a folder of product photos overnight may accept a heavier backbone if it improves quality. A live checkout camera that must react frame by frame often needs a lighter backbone so the product feels immediate.

cards.title: Fastest is not always best either
cards.body: It is just as wrong to say “always choose the smallest model.” If a task demands higher-quality features and latency is loose, a heavier backbone may be the better fit. The real question is whether the extra accuracy is worth the extra delay for that use case.

cards.title: Two ends of the tradeoff
cards.body: Neither side is universally better; the task decides what “good” means.

left.text:
Often stronger features
Usually more compute and latency
Useful when quality matters most

right.text:
Usually faster inference
Lower cost per prediction
Useful when response time matters most

exercises.prompt: When is a heavier backbone most defensible?

options.text: When you have looser latency limits and the extra feature quality matters for the task
When users need instant feedback on a tiny battery-powered device
When speed has no connection to user experience or cost
When you want to avoid feature extraction entirely

exercises.explain: Right — heavier backbones make the most sense when their extra cost is acceptable.

exercises.prompt: True or false: a lighter backbone is automatically the best choice for every vision system.
exercises.explain: False — speed helps, but some jobs justify paying for richer features.

exercises.sentence: Prediction ___ is the time it takes one model inference to finish.
exercises.explain: Exactly — latency is a key part of the accuracy-speed tradeoff.

exercises.prompt: Which product scenario most strongly pushes you toward a faster, lighter backbone?

options.text: A drone avoiding obstacles in real time while flying
An offline archive being processed overnight on a server
A printed manual sitting closed on a shelf

exercises.explain: Yes — real-time control systems feel latency immediately.

exercises.prompt: A retail team is building a live shelf scanner. Option A is slightly more accurate but noticeably slower. Option B is a bit less accurate but keeps the video stream feeling smooth. Which choice is most likely better for the product?

options.text: Option B, because real-time products often need fast enough predictions to preserve the user experience
Option A, because any slower model is automatically better if accuracy rises at all
Neither, because vision models cannot trade speed for accuracy
Either, because deployment environment never changes latency needs

exercises.explain: Exactly. In real-time systems, usable speed is part of success. Next: backbone choice also depends on where the model runs — in the cloud or on the edge.
